claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask some questions about your child (or children).

**Let's start with the basics:**

1. **How old is your child?** (If you have multiple children, let's start with one and we can assess each of them)

2. **What is your child's name?** (This will help me keep track if you have multiple children to assess)

The age of your child is important because:
- Children under 16 may automatically qualify
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over generally don't qualify

Please share these details, and we'll work through the eligibility assessment together.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Yes, I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. 

I have one child - Alex, who's 17 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old.

Since Alex is 17 (which falls in the 16-19 age range), their eligibility will depend on whether they're in approved education or training. Let me navigate to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to understand Alex's current education or training status.

**Is Alex currently:**

1. **In approved education or training?** (This could include full-time non-advanced education like A-levels, certain vocational qualifications, or approved training programs)

2. **Recently left education or training?** (within the last 20 weeks)

3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave

Please let me know which situation applies to Alex, and I can guide you through the next steps of the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex recently left education actually. They finished school and have been working with the local careers service to figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying! Since Alex recently left education, we need to check if they're eligible for an extension period. Child Benefit can sometimes continue for up to 20 weeks after a young person leaves education, under certain circumstances.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! This is relevant to your situation. There is a 20-week extension period available for young people aged 16-17 who have left approved education or training.

**To qualify for this extension, I need to check two things:**

1. **Has Alex left education within the last 20 weeks?** (How long ago did they finish school?)

2. **Has Alex registered with a qualifying service?** This includes:
   - A government careers service
   - Connexions (career development service)
   - The armed services

You mentioned Alex has been working with the local careers service - this is a good sign! 

**Please confirm:**
- When did Alex leave education? (to ensure it's within the 20-week period)
- Is Alex registered with the careers service (not just receiving informal help, but officially registered)?

The extension would end at week 20 after leaving education OR when Alex turns 18, whichever comes sooner.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex left school a few months back - it's been about 12 weeks now. And yes, they're properly registered with the careers service, not just having a chat - they've signed up officially to get help sorting out their next steps.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! That's really helpful information. So Alex:
- Left school 12 weeks ago (which is within the 20-week extension period)
- Is officially registered with the careers service (which is a qualifying service)

This means Alex qualifies for the extension period. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Alex's work situation.

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week?** (or not in paid work at all?)

This is important because young people aged 16 or over who are working 24 hours or more per week (and not in approved education/training) are not eligible for Child Benefit.

What is Alex's current work situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at all right now. They're just focusing on sorting out what they want to do next with the careers service.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex isn't working at all, that meets this requirement. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about benefits.

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right?** Specifically:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Or similar benefits claimed in their own name

Children who are receiving these types of benefits in their own right are not eligible for Child Benefit.

Is Alex receiving any of these benefits?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't receiving any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about apprenticeships.

**Is Alex currently doing an apprenticeship?**

The rules vary depending on the type and location:
- **England**: All apprenticeships (Intermediate, Advanced, Higher, Degree) would disqualify Alex from Child Benefit
- **Scotland**: Modern Apprenticeships would disqualify Alex
- **Wales**: Foundation Apprenticeships are approved and don't disqualify, but other apprenticeships do

So, is Alex:
1. **Not in an apprenticeship at all?**
2. **In a Foundation Apprenticeship in Wales?** (this would be fine)
3. **In any other type of apprenticeship?**

What's Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex hasn't started an apprenticeship. They're still figuring out what they want to do.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is not in an apprenticeship, that's fine. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status, as you need to meet UK residency requirements to claim Child Benefit.

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

This is usually straightforward - if you live in the UK as your main home, you meet this requirement.

Alternatively, there are other ways to qualify if you have:
- Settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- Pre-settled status (with certain conditions like working, studying, or having sufficient resources)
- Are living abroad as a Crown servant

What is your residency situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - this is my home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirement. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check if you are responsible for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can meet this requirement in several ways:

1. **Living with Alex** - Do you live together in the same household?

2. **Contributing to Alex's upkeep** - Even if not living together, do you pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent) towards their care? This includes things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents.

3. **Paying maintenance** - Under a court order or agreement that covers Alex's upkeep.

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex lives with you, you meet the responsibility requirement. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else who might be claiming

If someone else is claiming, it's important to know:
- Have they agreed to transfer the claim to you?
- Or is there a dispute about who should claim?

What's the situation - is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since no one else is claiming, let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to understand Alex's living situation more specifically.

**Where is Alex currently living?**

Please let me know which of these applies:

1. **In hospital** - Is Alex currently staying in hospital?
2. **In care** - Is Alex in local authority care?
3. **Fostered** - Is Alex in a foster care arrangement?
4. **Living normally at home with you** - Alex lives at home as part of your household

Which situation describes Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living normally at home with me - just a regular household situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex is living normally at home with you, let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Just one final check.

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

Generally, you cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child's upkeep.

Is the local council contributing financially towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex - I'm supporting them myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me process the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# 🎉 Great News! You Are Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE** ✓

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex!

## Why You're Eligible

Alex qualifies under the **20-week extension provision** for young people aged 16-17 who have recently left education. Here's what made you eligible:

✓ **Alex is 17 years old** and left school 12 weeks ago (within the 20-week extension period)  
✓ **Alex is officially registered** with the local careers service (a qualifying service)  
✓ **Alex is not working** 24+ hours per week  
✓ **Alex is not receiving benefits** in their own right  
✓ **Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship**  
✓ **You normally live in the UK**  
✓ **You live with Alex** and are responsible for them  
✓ **No one else is claiming** Child Benefit for Alex  
✓ **Alex lives normally at home** with you  
✓ **The local council is not paying** towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance

## Important Time Limit

Please note that this extension will end at **week 20 after Alex left education** (so you have approximately 8 weeks remaining) OR when Alex turns 18, **whichever comes sooner**. 

## Next Steps to Claim

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay back some or all of the benefit through tax
5. **Payments are made every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

## How to Apply

Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

If you have any questions about the application process or need clarification, please don't hesitate to ask!

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and left school 12 weeks ago, which is within the 20-week extension period. Alex is officially registered with the local careers service (a qualifying service), is not working (less than 24 hours per week), is not receiving benefits in their own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant normally lives in the UK, lives with Alex and is responsible for them, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met, including the special 20-week extension provision for 16-17 year olds who have recently left education and registered with a qualifying service.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 17-year-old child Alex under the 20-week extension provision. Alex recently left education 12 weeks ago and is registered with the careers service, meeting all requirements for continued eligibility during this transition period.

response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Has the child (aged 16-17) left education within the last 20 weeks AND registered with a qualifying service?
response.help_text: 20-week extension available if child has left approved education/training and registered with: government careers service, Connexions, or armed services. Extension ends at week 20 or when child turns 18, whichever is sooner.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
